coredata.dc:title: Terbium (III)-based Metallacrowns with aggregation-induced emission feature coupled with cu (II) for fluorescence detection of cysteine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Fei
Wang, Yue
Jiang, Dafeng
Zhao, Xian-en
coredata.dc:description: The Metallacrowns (MCs) composed of repeated [Metal-N-O] subunits are a type of new material, but the MCs have not been developed and utilized in analytical applications. This essay reports on a new kind of terbium(III)-based Metallacrowns (Tb-MCs) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) feature to build a sensing platform. It is first time that Tb-MCs are able to aggregate to larger aggregates in water along with a bright green emission, so that the property makes it possible to apply in biosensing. Thereafter, the AIE of Tb-MCs can be quenched effectively by Cu2+. Based on the high affinity of thiol to Cu2+, cysteine (Cys) recovers the fluorescence of Tb-MCs in the presence of Cu2+. There is a good linear range varying from 0.02 to 20 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) 9.67 nM of Cys. In the end, this novel probe is also successfully applied to the determination of Cys in human serum with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cysteine
Terbium(III)-based Metallacrowns
Cu2+
Aggregation-induced emission

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of Tb-MCs in different ratios of water
Determination of Cys in human serum
Detection of Cys in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of Tb-MCs in methanol (a); FT-IR spectra of sodium benzoate, H3shi and Tb-MCs (b); fluorescence spectra of free Tb3+, H3shi, and Tb-MCs (c); UV–vis absorption spectra of sodium benzoate (blue line), H3shi (red line) and Tb-MCs (black line) (d).
Fluorescence response of Tb-MCs to different ratio of water in water/methanol mixture (a); TEM images of Tb-MCs in water fraction of 10% (b); 50% (c) and 90% (v/v) (d) in water/methanol mixture.

Fluorescence spectra of Tb-MC aggregates in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ (a); the corresponding linear range of Cu2+ (b). The Y-axis is (F0-F1)/F0, in which F1 and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of Tb-MCs in the presence and absence of Cu2+, respectively.
Luminescence lifetime decay curves of Tb-MCs before and after the addition of Cu2+ ions.
Fluorescence spectra of Tb-MC aggregates in the absence and presence of Cu2+ (10 μM), Cys (20 μM), and the mixture of Cu2+ and Cys.

Fluorescence spectra of Tb-MCs in the presence of Cu2+ with addition of various concentrations of Cys (a); the corresponding linear range of Cys (b). The Y-axis is (F2-F1)/F1, where F1 and F2 represent the fluorescence intensity of Tb-MC-Cu2+ system in the absence and presence of Cys.

Selectivity towards Cys. The concentrations of Cys, ATP, and His are 20 μM. The concentrations of other substances are 40 μM and Cu2+ is 10 μM. The Y-axis is (F2′-F1)/F1, where F1 and F2′ represent the fluorescence intensity of Tb-MC-Cu2+ system in the absence and presence of interference substances.
Plausible energy transfer mechanism of Tb3+ ions in the absence (A) and presence (B) of the Cu2+ ions.
Schematic illustration of the detection of Cys based on Tb-MC-Cu2+ system.
Comparison of fluorescence methods involved Cu2+ for the detection of Cys.
Detection of Cys in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Terbium (III)-based Metallacrowns with aggregation-induced emission feature coupled with cu (II) for fluorescence detection of cysteine

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Metallacrowns (MCs) composed of repeated [Metal-N-O] subunits are a type of new material, but the MCs have not been developed and utilized in analytical applications. This essay reports on a new kind of terbium(III)-based Metallacrowns (Tb-MCs) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) feature to build a sensing platform. It is first time that Tb-MCs are able to aggregate to larger aggregates in water along with a bright green emission, so that the property makes it possible to apply in biosensing. Thereafter, the AIE of Tb-MCs can be quenched effectively by Cu2+. Based on the high affinity of thiol to Cu2+, cysteine (Cys) recovers the fluorescence of Tb-MCs in the presence of Cu2+. There is a good linear range varying from 0.02 to 20 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) 9.67 nM of Cys. In the end, this novel probe is also successfully applied to the determination of Cys in human serum with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: The Metallacrowns (MCs) are a kind of unique macrocyclic compounds with repeating [Metal-N-O] subunits, whose structures are analogous to organic crown ethers [1]. In usual, MCs coordinate the central metal cation relying on the oxygen binding sites of ligands [1]. Compounds based on hydroxamate are mostly used ligands and the majority of which are synthesized by the group of Vincent L. Pecoraro [2-4]. For instance, a kind of MCs was synthesized with Zn2+ as the MC ring metal, picoline hydroxamic acid as ligands and a single Ln3+ as a central ion [2]. What’s more, a second-generation MCs on the basis of quinaldic hydroxamic acid showed available emission bands in near-infrared (NIR) spectral regions owing to Yb3+ or Nd3+ ions as the luminescent center [3]. In recent years, MCs coordinated by pyrazine hydroxamic acid exhibited superb water solubility, and they were admirably suitable for optical biological imaging [4]. In spite the lanthanide-based MCs (Ln-MCs) show excellent optical properties, there has not been detailed study in other performances such as their response to external stimuli.

For another, cysteine (Cys) plays a vital part in protein synthesis, detoxification and metabolism [5]. When there is a lack of Cys, liver damage, loss of muscle, lethargy and weakness may be results [6]. Therefore, it is essential to detect Cys in a sensitive and accurate manner. As we know, the thiol (-SH) group of Cys can coordinate with metal ions in order to form the metal-S bond. As a consequence, two ways can be used to detect Cys sensitively. For one thing, the luminescence or the Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorbance of metallic nanomaterials can be changed by means of adding Cys. For example, a dual-emission probe containing carbon dots and gold nanoclusters was established, and then Cys quenched the fluorescence of gold nanoclusters rather than carbon dots on account of the strong Au-S interactions [7]. Likewise, Cys decreased the UV–vis absorbance of Ag/Pd bimetallic nanoparticles as there was a strong affinity between Ag and the -SH group in Cys [8]. For another, it can be achieved by regarding metal ions as signal transduction switch. Typically, the emission of a variety of nanoprobes such as carbon dots [9], quantum dots [10], and polymer nanoparticles [11] were reduced by Cu2+. But the affinity between Cu2+ and Cys is of greater power contributing to the recovery of fluorescence. In spite of this, the detection of Cys is usually disturbed by some substances such as Histidine (His) and adenosine triphosphate (ATP). It is because the binding between probes and metal ions is weaker, which makes it easier for Cu2+ to coordinates with ATP or His. Consequently, when probes combine with Cu2+ in a stronger way, the selectivity of the sensor can be remarkably improved.

In the study, the highly luminescent terbium(III)-based MCs (Tb-MCs) are prepared with an improvement in the synthesis, where salicylhydroxamic acid (H3shi) serves as ligand and gallium ions (Ga3+) is used as the ring metal. The Tb-MCs display a strong green fluorescence and favorable time stability in methanol. And even more interesting, as the proportion of water increasing from 0 to 90% in methanol/water system, Tb-MCs aggregate gradually leading to the enhancement of fluorescence. To our knowledge, it is the first time to discover the phenomenon of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) for MCs in water. What’s more, the AIE is quenched when copper ions are introduced into Tb-MC aggregates, while fluorescence recovers by adding Cys. Inspired by that, we have developed a novel strategy with more admirable sensitivity to Cys, the linear of which ranges from 0.02 to 20 μM. What should be pointed is that Tb-MC aggregates/Cu2+ system possesses more spectacular selectivity towards Cys than other amino acids and coexisting species such as His and ATP. This may be attributed to the stronger affinity between Cu2+ and the probe. Finally, the sensor has been successfully applied to detect Cys in human serum samples with satisfactory recoveries.

Gallium nitrate hydrate (Ga (NO3)3·xH2O), salicylhydroxamic acid (H3shi), sodium benzoate, Tb (NO)3·6H2O, copper sulfate anhydrous (CuSO4), sodium acetate anhydrous (CH3COONa), ATP and amino acids including Cys, aspartic acid (Asp), phenylalanine (Phe), alanine (Ala), threonine (Thr), tyrosine (Tyr), lysine (Lys), glycine (Gly), valine (Val), methionine (Met), leucine (Leu), isoleucine (Ile), arginine (Arg) and His were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co (Shanghai, China). The human serum samples were collected from healthy volunteers in the local hospital. The water used throughout all experiments was ultrapure. All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained on a Cary 300 Bio UV–vis spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out (via the KBr pellet method) using a Thermo Nicolet (Madison, WI, USA) Nexus 470 FT-IR ESP spectrometer. The pH values of solutions were measured by using a pH meter (Mettler Toledo FE20, Zurich, Switzerland). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were conducted by a JEM-2100PLUS (JEOL, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime was determined using a photo-counting system from Quantaurus-Tau C11367–11 (Hamamatsu). The zeta potential was analyzed using a Zetasizer Nano ZS-90 (Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK).

The Tb-MCs were synthesized with an improvement according to a reported method [12]. In brief, Tb (NO)3·6H2O (10.9 mg, 0.025 mmol), Ga (NO)3·xH2O (25.6 mg, 0.1 mmol) and H3shi (15.3 mg, 0.1 mmol) were dissolved in 16 mL of methanol. After stirring for 10 min, sodium benzoate (43.2 mg, 0.3 mmol) was added to the solution and the mixture was stirred overnight. Then 200 μL pyridine were added and the resulting solution was stirred for 15 min and then centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min. Besides, the resulting solution was dried to obtain solid powder and weighed. After diluting to an appropriate concentration (6.0 mg/mL) with methanol, the final solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of Tb-MCs in different ratios of water
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the fluorescence response of Tb-MCs in water, we mixed different volumes of water and methanol, in which there were 100 μL stock solutions of Tb-MCs (6.0 mg/mL). The final volumes of the mixtures were 1.0 mL and the water content varied from 0% to 90%. After incubation for 10 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were obtained at an excitation wavelength of 345 nm.

For Cys detection, 100 μL solutions with different concentrations of Cys were premixed with 755 μL water, 100 μL Cu2+ (100 μmol/L) for 30 min. After that, 45 μL Tb-MCs (6.0 mg/mL) were added into the mixture and a 15 min incubation time was followed. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 345 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cys in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, the human serum samples were deproteinized by adding acetonitrile (the volume ratio of acetonitrile and plasma was 2:1) and centrifuging at 4000 rpm for 40 min at room temperature. Later, the supernatant was kept at 80 °C in a water bath for about 1 h to remove acetonitrile. Then, the supernatant was collected and diluted with deionized water to achieve the appropriate concentration to use. Subsequently, the concentration of Cys in serum samples was measured by means of the method in section 2.5. Then, three different concentrations of Cys standard solution were added into the samples and measured by the mentioned method.

There is a typical framework of Tb-MCs (structure in Fig. S1), where four Ga3+ ions are bridged by four salicylhydroximate (shi3−) ligands in a nearly planar geometry, and the central Tb3+ ion is bridged to the ring by four benzoate ligands [1]. In methanol, a pyridinium cation balances the negative charge of Tb-MCs, and it was found to come into being a hydrogen bond with an adjacent pyridine solvent molecule [1], so there is a good dispersibility in methanol for the Tb-MCs. In addition, they exhibit an approximate sphere morphology in TEM image in methanol and with diameters ranging from 0.25 to 0.5 μm (Fig. 1 a). The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1b) show that the strong feature bands of sodium benzoate at 1596–1546 and 1405 cm−1 are attributed to COO– stretching vibration, and the bands at 518 cm−1 are ascribed to Na-O bond (O1s-s*) [12]. However, they disappear in the spectrum of Tb-MCs, suggesting the formation of M−O linkages (M = Ga3+, Tb3+). The strong bands of H3shi at 3288 and 3129 cm−1 ascribed to the stretching vibration of N-H and O-H [13] also disappear in the spectrum of Tb-MCs, in keeping with the deprotonation of H3shi [14]. These results are consistent with the structure of the metallacrown ring formed through the (M−N−O-) n linkage [15]. Besides, the free Tb3+ ions have weak emissions due to the forbidden f-f transition [1] and the fluorescence of H3shi is at 423 nm. When these Tb-MCs are successfully synthesized, these nanoparticles emit bright fluorescence in methanol. Four unique emission peaks corresponding to the 5D4→7FJ (J = 6–0) transitions of Tb3+ are observed clearly at 496, 551, 585 and 623 nm upon the excitation at 345 nm (Fig. 1c), and the emission of H3shi disappears. The reason is that Tb-MCs are sensitized through the “antenna effect” with the excitation of the organic chromophoric moieties by electromagnetic radiation, and then the corresponding energy is transferred to the accepting levels of the Tb3+ ions [1]. Furthermore, the emissions of Tb-MCs at 496 and 551 nm are stronger than those at 585 nm and 623 nm, so the better ones are used for the further study. Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the sodium benzoate, H3shi and Tb-MCs are presented in Fig. 1d. The characteristic absorption band of H3shi at 300 nm and that of the sodium benzoate at 265 nm are an intraligand π → π* transition derived from aromatic structure [16]. The Tb-MCs includes these two absorption bands at 265 and 310 nm respectively, and the latter one is slightly red shift compared with H3shi. The reason is the formation of multiple rings in the Tb-MCs, which enhances the conjugate effect and reduces the energy required for electronic transitions, leading to the red shift of the absorption bands [17].

To explore the feasibility of sensing in the physiological condition, the impact of water on the fluorescence of Tb-MCs is investigated (Fig. 2 a). The Tb-MCs can disperse well in pure methanol with high emission. However, the fluorescence firstly decreases as water fractions ranges from 0 to 50 % (v/v) in water/methanol mixture. This may be attributed to the high energy O-H vibrations of H2O molecule, leading to the nonradiative deactivation and florescence quenching [1]. Interestingly, when the ratio of water rises from 50% to 60%, the fluorescence enhances significantly, and then it keeps a high level in the water fraction of 60%-90%. Moreover, TEM indicates that the spherical Tb-MCs start to aggregate at the water fraction of 10% (Fig. 2b). As the fraction of water increases to 50% and to 90%, the aggregates become larger and denser (Fig. 2c, d). DLS analyses validate that the hydrodynamic diameter of these Tb-MCs increases from 68.1 nm to 0.95 μm and then to 1.48 μm corresponding to the water fraction of 0%, 50% and 90% (v/v), respectively (Fig. S2). The aggregation of Tb-MCs is related to the low water solubility of shi3- [18], and the hydrogen bonding among the Tb-MCs also benefits the aggregation in water. In the aggregates, the intramolecular vibration may be strongly restrained, so the rates of radiative energy transfer increase by suppressing nonradiative relaxation of excited states [19]. However, more work is needed to unambiguously elucidate the AIE mechanism of Tb-MCs.

Besides, the AIE of Tb-MCs in water is stable in the pH range from 4 to 8, and this is beneficial for the sensing application (Fig. S3). In general, this is the first report about the AIE of lanthanide-based MCs, and it may pave the way for their development and application in biological sensing.

The impact of metal ions, including Cu2+, K+, Na+, Ag+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Fe3+, on the AIE of Tb-MC aggregates is explored. As depicted in Fig. S4, these metal ions have slight influence on Tb-MC aggregates, while the addition of Cu2+ quenches the fluorescence significantly. The quenching degree of Tb-MC aggregates is linearly proportional to the concentration of Cu2+ from 0.08 to 50 μM and the limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 55.5 nM (Fig. 3 a and 3b). LOD is the concentration of analyte that corresponds to three times the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N = 3) [3], LOD = 3σ/k, where 3σ is the standard deviation of the blank samples (n = 10), and k is the slope of the corresponding linear curve of the analyte.

Subsequently, fluorescence quenching mechanism of the Tb-MCs caused by Cu2+ is explored. It is known that transition metal ions, particularly Cu2+ ions, are able to quench Ln3+ luminescence [20]. The energy levels of Tb3+ [21] and Cu2+ [22] are acquired from the literature. As illustrated by Scheme 1 , Tb3+ are excited to the 5D1 state upon 345 nm excitation, and then the energy from this state is transferred to the 1D2 level of Cu2+ because 5D1 level is close to the 1D2 level. Later the de-excitation of the Cu2+ to the ground state occurs in nonradiative manner [20]. Therefore, the energy transfer mechanism is proposed to explain the fluorescence quenching. The decay curves suggest that the lifetime is 0.36 ms for the Tb-MC aggregates and then it reduces to 0.28 ms upon the addition of Cu2+ (Fig. 4 ), which supports the energy transfer mechanism well. Besides, the zeta potential of Tb-MCs is −21 mV at pH 6.0, and the Cu2+ is positively charged, indicating that the electrostatic attraction provides a possibility for energy transfer at a close range between Tb-MCs and Cu2+.

In Table 1 , various fluorescence methods for the detection of Cys involved Cu2+ have been compared. In these ways, the fluorescence of probes is quenched by Cu2+ on account of energy transfer [25], ligand–metal charge transfer process [26] and so on. Copper ions play an essential role in sensitive detection of Cys in fluorescence methods based on the high affinity of thiol to Cu2+ [11,25-30]. However, the LODs of Cys reported in these methods are not satisfactory. The reason may be related to the insensitive response of probe to Cu2+. Herein, benefited from the good response sensitivity of Tb-MCs to Cu2+, the LOD of Cys is as low as 9.67 nM, which is better than most reported studies for Cys detection involving Cu2+.

In order to study the reversibility of Tb-MCs to Cu2+ and Tb-MC-Cu2+ to Cys, the fluorescence intensity of Tb-MCs is measured by alternating addition of Cu2+ and Cys. As displayed in Fig. S8, the fluorescence of Tb-MCs is quenched upon addition of Cu2+ and then it enhances with the addition of Cys. The reversible cycle can be repeated at least 4 times with little fluorescence efficiency loss under the same conditions. The results indicate that the reversibility of Tb-MCs to Cu2+ and Tb-MC-Cu2+ to Cys is preferable. The good reversibility is essential for the potential application.

The interferences from several common substances, including Ala, Met, Gly, Arg, Lys, Leu, Thr, Asp, Val, Ile, Tyr, Phe, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, NH4 +, ATP, His, for the selectivity of Tb-MC-Cu2+ system towards Cys have been investigated. Based on the high affinity between Cu2+ and Cys, only Cys induces the distinct fluorescent recovery of Tb-MC-Cu2+ system, while other substances cannot (Fig. 7 and Fig. S9). Especially, the His and ATP have slight influence on the detection of Cys. Because the binding force between Cu2+ and the probe is stronger than that of ATP and His, they have little interference in the detection of Cys. Therefore, the interference substances cannot influence the detection of Cys in this strategy, indicating a high selectivity towards Cys.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cys in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The measurement of Cys is implemented in human serum samples for the sake of assessing the practicability of the presented strategy. As shown in Table 2 , different amounts of Cys of standard solutions are spiked into the diluted serum samples. From all the above, it turns out to be satisfactory recoveries in the range of 96.73%-104.97% with relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.23%-3.51%, manifesting that this method is reliable to analyze Cys in real samples.

Herein, based on the AIE of Tb-MCs in the water and the quenching effect by Cu2+, a new type of sensing platform for Cys detection has been developed. The main advantages of this assay exist in three ways. (1) It is discovered first that the Tb-MCs possess the property of AIE in water. (2) The application of Tb-MCs in the aspect of detecting biomolecules is firstly studied. (3) The Tb-MC-Cu2+ system exhibits a better sensitivity compared with other assays for determination of Cys with Cu2+ on account of the strong binding between Tb-MCs and Cu2+. In conclusion, this new strategy is meaningful as not only does it provide new possibilities for Cys detection, but it offers a new avenue to expand the applications of Tb-MCs with AIE feature in biological applications.

This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2019QB010), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22176109) and the Scientific Research Foundation of Qufu Normal University (BSQD20130117).

The human serum sample experiments were performed in accordance with the guidelines from the Ethical Committee, Qufu Normal University. All serum samples were obtained from healthy volunteers with their informed consent. All studies were approved by the Ethical Committee of Qufu Normal University.
Fei Qu and Yue Wang contributed equally to this work.

Fei Qu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yue Wang: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Dafeng Jiang: Supervision, Project administration. Xian-en Zhao: Supervision, Project administration, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121181.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ga3+/Ln3+ metallacrowns: a promising family of highly luminescent lanthanide complexes that covers visible and near-infrared domains
J. Am. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chow CY, Eliseeva SV, Trivedi ER, Tu NN, Kampf JW, Petoud S, Pecoraro VL. Ga3+/Ln3+ Metallacrowns: A promising family of highly luminescent lanthanide complexes that covers visible and near-infrared domains. J Am Chem Soc. 2016, 138:5100-5109. Doi: 10.1021/jacs.6b00984.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Assembly of near-infrared luminescent lanthanide host (host-guest) complexes with a Metallacrown sandwich motif
Angew. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jankolovits J, Andolina CM, Kampf JW, Raymond KN, Pecoraro VL. Assembly of near-infrared luminescent lanthanide host (host-guest) complexes with a Metallacrown sandwich motif. Angew. Chem. 2011,123:9834-9838. Doi: 10.1002/ange.201103851.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly emitting near-infrared lanthanide “encapsulated sandwich” metallacrown complexes with excitation shifted toward lower energy
J. Am. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Trivedi ER, Eliseeva SV, Jankolovits J, Olmstead MM, Petoud S, Pecoraro VL. Highly emitting near-infrared lanthanide “encapsulated sandwich” metallacrown complexes with excitation shifted toward lower energy. J Am Chem Soc. 2014, 136:1526-1534. Doi: 10.1021/ja4113337.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stephane Petoud. Near-infrared optical imaging of necrotic cells by photostable lanthanide-based metallacrowns
J Am Chem Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ivana Martinic, Svedana V. Eliseeva, NN Tu, VL Pecoraro, Stephane Petoud. Near-infrared optical imaging of necrotic cells by photostable lanthanide-based metallacrowns. J Am Chem Soc. 2017, 139:8388-8391. Doi: 10.1021/jacs.7b01587.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly selective two-photon fluorescent probe for ratiometric sensing and imaging cysteine in mitochondria
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Niu WF, Guo L, Li YH, Shuang SM, Dong C, and Wong MS. Highly selective two-photon fluorescent probe for ratiometric sensing and imaging cysteine in mitochondria. Anal Chem. 2015, 88: 1908-1914. Doi: 10.1021/acs.analchem.5b04329.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanosensor composed of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and gold nanoparticles for highly selective detection of cysteine with multiple signals
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Deng JH, Lu QJ, Hou YX, Liu ML, Li HT, Zhang YY, Yao SZ. Nanosensor composed of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and gold nanoparticles for highly selective detection of cysteine with multiple signals. Anal Chem. 2015, 87:2195-2203. Doi: 10.1021/ac503595y.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The construction of a FRET assembly by using gold nanoclusters and carbon dots and their application as a ratiometric probe for cysteine detection
Sensors Actuators B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yu X, Zhang CX, Zhang L, Xue YR, Li HW, Wu YQ. The construction of a FRET assembly by using gold nanoclusters and carbon dots and their application as a ratiometric probe for cysteine detection. Sensors Actuators B Chem. 2018, 263: 327-335. Doi: 10.1016/j.snb.2018.02.072.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive and selective colorimetric probe for determination of l-cysteine in aqueous media based on Ag/Pd bimetallic nanoparticles
Sensors Actuators B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Farhadi K, Forough M, Pourhossein A, Molaei R. Highly sensitive and selective colorimetric probe for determination of l-cysteine in aqueous media based on Ag/Pd bimetallic nanoparticles. Sensors Actuators B Chem. 2014, 202: 993-1001. Doi: 10.1016/j.snb.2014.05.129.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as fluorescent probes for “off–on” detection of Cu2+ and l-cysteine in aqueous solution
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zong J, Yang XL, Adrian T, Simon H, Ivan C, Zhu YH, Li CZ, Tim M, Wei G. Carbon dots as fluorescent probes for “off–on” detection of Cu2+ and l-cysteine in aqueous solution. Biosens. Bioelectron. 2014, 51: 330-335. Doi: 10.1016/j.bios.2013.07.042.

Kumar, Sensitive and selective turn-off-on fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and cysteine using nitrogen doped carbon nanodots derived from citron and urine, Sensors Actuators B Chem. 259 (2018) 1133–1143, doi: 10.1016/j.snb.2017.12.046.
